new_findings.title: Use a tiered 'script' — escalating PPV ladder from $15 to $200+ per session

variants.claim: A pre-built escalation script that guides the fan through progressively more explicit content at increasing price points generates $300–$500 per subscriber per session vs. a one-off $50 custom video.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters use a fixed script structure: (1) open with small talk / lifestyle warmup, (2) send 1–2 free teaser images to raise arousal, (3) transition to selling with a tiered video ladder — e.g., $15 (tease only) → $30 (slightly more) → $60 (light action) → $120 (more explicit) → $200 (full action). Content explicitness scales with price. Subscriber buys each step sequentially; by the final video, the session is complete and they've spent $300–$500. The same script is reused across all subscribers.

variants.claim: A pre-made DM script that sequences plain messages, half-nude images, and full-nude PPV in a deliberate ladder is the core chatting monetization engine.
variants.what_they_do: Agency writes a reusable script sequence: opener message → follow-up message → half-nude image send → bridging message → full explicit PPV offer → continuation. Recommends doing 6–7 hours of chatting personally first to learn the craft before hiring chatters. Treats this as the primary revenue driver (80–90% of OF revenue).

variants.claim: Starting PPV with a free non-revealing image, then a small $5–15 paid image, then escalating to $30–40+ video creates a progressive upsell funnel that qualifies spenders before committing to expensive content.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter sends (1) a free, non-revealing image to open the conversation; (2) a slightly more revealing image/clip priced $5–15 to qualify whether the fan will spend; (3) subsequent escalating PPV pieces priced $30–40+. Welcome/intro message is auto-sent at $15–25 on subscribe. Each step is treated as a 'drink at the bar' — the fan self-selects into a higher spend tier organically.

variants.claim: Sending a free teaser photo followed by a scripted sequence of PPV content with escalating price points ($15, $50, $100, $150, $200) converts fans into high spenders by building investment and desire incrementally.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters follow a pre-built content flow the creator has shot in advance. Conversation starts organically, then a free clothed photo is sent. First PPV is priced low ($15–20, ~15–20 second video). Free content interspersed between paid unlocks to maintain engagement. Each subsequent PPV increases in price and explicitness. A single fan session can yield $300–400. Chatters run 24/7 in 8-hour rotating shifts (3 chatters per creator account), sourced primarily from South Africa for native English and cultural fluency.

variants.claim: After the first PPV purchase, cycling between descriptive text exchange, free teasers, and the next PPV sell maximizes session revenue; stopping the moment the fan finishes or runs out of money protects the long-term relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Post-first-purchase loop: (1) ask 'what would you do now if you saw me like this?' to get descriptive text, (2) mirror his fantasy back, (3) insert free teaser (photo/voice/short clip — not enough to satisfy), (4) set up next PPV with yes-ladder, (5) sell. Repeat until fan orgasms or explicitly says no money. At that point, immediately pivot to relationship-building mode — pre-recorded 'had a great time' appreciation video, romantic post-session questions.

sources.quote: you have the first video for like $15 second video for 30 then for 60 for 120 for 200... you just continuously progress
if he buys an entire script that's about $300 to $500 you make per subscriber and that's how your channel goes to the big big levels
you want to make one message, then another, then maybe send her a half nude image, then another message, then a full nude, then blah blah blah

the next video or picture will be a little more revealing you'll sell that for five to ten dollars and so on and so forth the next one from there will be 30 to 40
the first ppv messages should be pictures that are like five to ten dollars you're just trying to qualify if this guy is a spender or a Time waster
the price goes from $15 to 50, then to 100, then to $150, then to $200, and before you know it, the person has spent maybe $300, $400
descriptive text, sound turned on, get descriptive text from him, make him imagine the scenario in his head, send free teasers, set up the next PPV, and sell the PPV
Sell until you can sell, right? Until he's tapped out or he comes.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing ladder or chat script structure (§7 GAP, §8 OUTSOURCED). No internal baseline exists for escalation sequencing. This is high-value build-toward intel for both the in-house chat team and AI chatting projects.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a canonical escalation script template for chatters: define 5–6 tiers with explicit price points, content-reveal thresholds, and transition copy at each step. Treat it as a reusable asset deployed across all creators. Price anchoring should start low ($15) to minimize entry friction. Prioritize this for the AI chatting project where scripts can be fully automated.

new_findings.title: Four-axis sub qualification (profession, location, age, hobbies) to size wallet and build rapport

variants.claim: Systematically qualifying subscribers on four data points — profession, location, age, and hobbies — lets chatters estimate spending capacity and tailor their communication style before attempting to sell.
variants.what_they_do: After the personalized opener gets a response, chatters move into a structured qualification sequence. They ask about: (1) Profession — men spend 40+ hrs/week on work and will talk about it freely; also signals income level. (2) Location — dictates tone, cultural references, and timezone. (3) Age — a 52-year-old has 30 more years of earning history than a 22-year-old; older subs get more formal language, younger subs get slang. (4) Hobbies — reveals personality and gives commonality-building material. Chatters are also taught 'level two qualification' — drilling one level deeper on each topic (e.g., 'How did you get into that job?') to extract more information and signal genuine interest.

variants.claim: Selling is most effective when chatters first collect structured fan data (name, location, age, job, kinks) to gauge spending power and build connection, instead of spamming PPVs on entry.
variants.what_they_do: Uses systemized new-subscriber scripts with pre-written replies so every chatter sounds identical to the model, collects data to estimate purchasing power (e.g. country/job), records it in per-fan notes, and only escalates to a PPV ladder that builds up to ~$200.

variants.claim: Talking to a fan before quoting any price lets chatters learn his job/lifestyle and set a higher first PPV price ($25 vs $10) because no anchor was established early.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters ask open questions about job and hobbies — not from a script, but organically — to extract spending-power signals. Light flirting and jokes are used; sexual escalation is avoided until the fan initiates. The chatter never names a price before this rapport window closes.

sources.quote: number one profession you will be talking to men okay simps Subs they are men
another one is age how old are they part of the reason of qualifying is that we want to see their potential as a customer
what's going to make you money is data, right? Data collection
you can get the scripts all the way up to $200
we do use scripts inside our agency, but we never have the rapport part scripted as it's really important to let the conversation run smoothly
you're also going to get a glimpse of his spending power. So for how much you can send him PPVs

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline at Palm for chatter qualification frameworks (§8, outsourced). Build-toward for in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a qualification checklist into the in-house chatter training SOP: chatters must capture profession, location, age, and at least one hobby for every sub before escalating to selling. Store this data per-fan in the Fan Tracker CRM (§9) to enable personalized re-engagement. Age and profession data should also feed the whale-hunting logic — a 50-year-old professional is a higher-priority cultivation target than an 18-year-old student.

new_findings.title: Promote staff up the five levels of accountability to remove yourself as bottleneck

variants.claim: Scale past a plateau by training department heads to own problems end-to-end rather than escalating every decision to the owner.
variants.what_they_do: Five accountability levels: (1) wait to be told, (2) bring the problem, (3) bring problem + 1-2 solutions, (4) solve it and report, (5) solve it AND build an SOP so it never recurs. Most teams sit at 1-2, making the owner the bottleneck. Push C-suite heads to level 4-5; example: an acquisition manager independently bought aged warmed IG accounts and hired another VA to lift outreach capacity, then just reported it. Hold Mon-Fri morning team calls with only the department heads.

variants.claim: Founders scale out of the business by delegating outcomes and decision-making (an ownership ladder), not by dumping tasks, which only deepens dependency.
variants.what_they_do: Audits dollar-value-per-hour (only hires when a low-DVPH task blocks a high one), develops people up a level 1-5 accountability dial from doer to owner, and installs a decision filter so nobody escalates a problem without bullets + 2-3 solutions + a recommendation; uses the '30-day disappear test' to find the ceiling.

variants.claim: Heavy supervision layers and clear workflows let the founder step away while the business keeps running, which is the real test of a scaled agency.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media (300 employees, 60 models) layers supervisors into category managers into shift/market managers per department, and the founder says if he disappeared for six months the company would still run profitably.

sources.quote: there are five main levels of accountability... the first level being they just simply wait for the command on what to do.
what's different between level five and level four is they not only find the problem and solve it, but they also will build infrastructure to ensure that this problem never happens again.
making sure that you guys do morning team calls Monday to Friday.
delegation actually works is if you delegate outcomes plus decision-making instead of delegating tasks
never bring me a problem without what happened... 2-3 solutions, your recommendation
if I disappear for 6 months right now, will my company grow? Maybe not exponentially, but it will run

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no hiring scorecards, accountability framework, or management cadence documented (§10 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt the five-level accountability ladder as a management standard, push leads to level 4-5 with SOP-building expectations, and run Mon-Fri morning leadership calls.

new_findings.title: Make the fan chase to hold power

variants.claim: Letting the fan initiate and withholding rather than over-giving keeps the model in the power position and increases buying.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard plays hot-and-cold and sends non-nude teasers so the customer initiates, putting the chatter in the power position; habibi withholds (tells a fan asking for photos 'no, you're going to have to wait') to drive desire before selling.

variants.claim: Deliberately breaking rapport with playful negative statements (e.g., 'I was starting to like you until you said that') and push-pull phrasing prevents the sub from losing interest and causes them to actively seek the chatter's validation — which is the precondition for purchasing.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are drilled on two borrowed-from-pickup techniques: (1) Rapport breaks — any statement that actively discourages the deepening of connection, always softened as a joke (e.g., 'you and I would never get along because you're a math teacher and I have such trauma about math teachers'). These are not standalone — they must be followed up or contextualized so they don't read as mean over text. (2) Push-pulls — a positive statement immediately followed by a negative, or vice versa (e.g., 'you're actually kind of funny and I bet people teased you for it growing up'). These are to be peppered throughout the entire conversation, not saved for one moment. The psychological goal: sub chases chatter's validation, which increases desire to please — i.e., spend money.

variants.claim: Chatters should never directly initiate sexual conversation — instead, send an ambiguous teaser that makes sexual escalation the fan's idea, reducing friction and preserving the illusion of authenticity.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter sends a low-reveal teaser (e.g., model in a towel with wet hair, caption: 'just got out of the shower') with no sexual framing. The fan reads the signal and responds sexually — he has technically initiated. Alternatively, a 3-second clip of the model waving to camera with a self-deprecating caption ('my face looks so bad right now') achieves the same passive initiation. Once the fan escalates, the chatter follows his lead and layers in a second, slightly more revealing teaser before setting up the first PPV.

sources.quote: we always want to let the customer initiate... we are the ones that are in the power position
even if a guy says hey can you send me photos of your tits you just say simply no you're going to have to wait
a break in rapport anything that actively discourages the deepening or establishment of a positive connection
you should be doing push pulls you should really be doing breaks and rapport or specifically push pulls throughout the entire interaction
the most important part here is to let him initiate a sexual conversation

palm_comparison.vs_us: Outsourced chatting; Palm owns no objection/power-dynamic playbook (§8 OUTSOURCED). Build-toward. Cross-creator consensus (Luca + habibi).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Encode push-pull and let-the-fan-initiate tactics into the in-house chatting SOP rather than leaving tone to the vendor.

new_findings.title: Girlfriend-experience parasocial selling, not PPV spam

variants.claim: The girlfriend experience (parasocial connection, scripted live-feeling sells, no PPV spam) drives the highest and most durable chatting revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Optimizes the OF wall to feel like 'Snapchatting,' uses 7-10 stage scripts with verification images, never initiates sexual content (lets the fan initiate), handles broke fans with ego-protecting lines, and forbids PPV spamming across the whole team.

variants.claim: DM selling is treated as gamified casino psychology where the chatter maintains the power dynamic, captures fan data for rapport, and never breaks the fantasy by talking price.
variants.what_they_do: Auto-welcome DM collects name/age/interests into fan notes for later rapport (asking about a fan's dog by name); avoid being beggy or discussing price; price-locked content keeps fans from noticing spend; keep the model 'the prize' and just out of reach; deny AI accusations and use new content plus voice notes to rebut.

variants.claim: Subscribers don't spend money on OF because they can't find free explicit content; they spend because they're buying an emotional, intimate experience that free sites can't replicate.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are trained to frame every interaction as building genuine intimacy — shyness, excitement, escalating personal connection — rather than a content transaction. The chatter script is designed so the fan feels like the experience is happening *with* the creator, not being sold a product. The script's pacing (slow → fast) mirrors real intimacy. This psychological framing is positioned as the core reason scripted chats outperform both mass PPVs and one-off custom content.

sources.quote: the GF experience, the only thing that I try to run in OFM
in a casino they do everything to make you blind to the fact you're there losing all your money
use fan notes to store this information
only fans isn't about sexuality it's about emotions and experiences
what he really searches for are the emotions the real sexual experience not just the pure sexuality

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is OUTSOURCED at Palm with no in-house SOP (§8). This is direct build-toward intelligence for Palm's planned in-house and AI chat teams.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture the GFE framework (wall optimization, staged scripts, verification, no-spam rule) as the seed SOP for Palm's future in-house/AI chatting.

new_findings.topic: Reddit LTV vs. other traffic sources
new_findings.title: Reddit organic NSFW traffic produces the highest fan LTV of any channel

variants.claim: Fans acquired through niche-matched NSFW Reddit posts have significantly higher LTV than Instagram or dating-app traffic because they self-selected into a subreddit specifically for their fetish and actively chose to view the content, indicating high purchase intent.
variants.what_they_do: Agency benchmarks LTV by traffic source. Reddit organic NSFW: $8–$35 LTV on free pages. Dating app traffic also historically high LTV. Instagram traffic lower. Agency prioritises Reddit despite declining ROI trend because subscriber quality (intentionality) remains superior. At peak (3 years ago) one viral SFW post could drive 3,000–5,000 subs/day; currently realistic target is 10+ paid subs/day per account on a well-matched creator.

variants.claim: Reddit traffic must be run manually with niche-matched content and captions per subreddit because, unlike IG/X, the poster is effectively the algorithm choosing the audience.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media keeps 100 manual Reddit VAs (each issued a physical phone within four weeks) matching niche content and captioning to specific subreddits while fully automating IG/X/Threads; Reddit free subs cost $0.25-$1 and return $4-$8.

variants.claim: Outsourcing Reddit and X content distribution to a specialist contractor can yield disproportionate revenue relative to cost (cited: $1K/month spend generating ~$20K/month in revenue).
variants.what_they_do: Agency pays a Reddit/X specialist contractor roughly $1,000/month to distribute content across relevant subreddits and X accounts, funneling traffic to OF. The contractor is found through the agency's Telegram network. ROI is tracked as revenue attributed to that traffic source versus contractor cost.

sources.quote: we do see a way way higher LTV from these fans off of Reddit, off of the regular Reddit strategy than we see from Instagram fans
I think it's safe to say that that's the best way to get the highest um highest quality of subscriber to the account
On Reddit, you are the algorithm essentially. You are determining which subreddits
cost per acquisition of a Reddit subscriber is anywhere from 25 cents to a dollar... getting anywhere from $4 to $8 a return
I pay him like a thousand bucks a month and he brings me like $20,000 a month
your Reddit and X contractors are distributing content and funneling into loads of subs turning into hefty profit

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented traffic-source LTV benchmarking (§9 GAP — no written retention/churn strategy). Palm's whale-hunting dashboard (§9) tracks per-fan spend but does not appear to attribute fans to acquisition channel. This LTV signal is highly actionable for Palm's channel prioritisation decisions.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add acquisition-channel tagging to Palm's Fan Tracker CRM (§9) so LTV can be broken down by source (IG reel, Reddit, etc.). Even before launching Reddit, build the measurement infra so that if/when Reddit is tested, LTV comparison data is captured from day one. Use the $8–$35 Reddit LTV benchmark as the hurdle rate when evaluating Reddit's cost-per-acquisition against account farming costs.

new_findings.title: Multi-stage application filter: attention check + AP score + template effort scoring

variants.claim: Embedding a small compliance instruction in the job post (e.g. 'write I understand at the bottom') combined with checking onlinejobs.ph Application Points and requiring a filled template eliminates ~40–50% of low-quality applicants before any interview.
variants.what_they_do: Three-layer pre-screen: (1) embed a hidden instruction in the job post requiring a specific phrase to prove they read it; (2) on onlinejobs.ph, prioritize applicants who spent more Application Points (higher AP = higher motivation, lower AP = mass-applying); (3) send all applicants a structured template (name, age, experience, English level, open-ended questions like 'describe your communication style') and filter out anyone who answers lazily or skips fields.

variants.claim: A slow, multi-stage hiring funnel with strict filtering criteria is cheaper than hiring the wrong person, whose true cost in lost time, training, and mistakes runs into tens of thousands of dollars.
variants.what_they_do: Agency runs a dedicated hiring funnel lasting roughly one week: job post → multiple filtering stages → skills/personality tests → pick only the single best-qualified applicant. If no applicant clears the bar, the owner does the job themselves for another 2 weeks and re-runs the funnel rather than settling. Once hired, the employee gets a fully thought-through onboarding, individual KPI tracking, and formal performance reviews.

variants.claim: Replacing ad-hoc 'hire and hope' with a structured vetting process — Google Form application, defined training SOPs, and clear role expectations — results in hires who are productive from day one.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin's system uses Google Forms for initial applicant screening (filtering for baseline competence and fit before any call), paired with written training SOPs so hired VAs/chatters know exactly what to do from day one without hand-holding. Barry specifically credits a VA hired through this system as being immediately effective. The process is described as straightforward but most operators never implement it.

sources.quote: put a simple instruction next to the job post. So, if you apply, text me I understand at the bottom of your application
you always want to look at the AP... if there's an application with just one AP, one application point, means he probably sent it to like a thousand other agencies
they should fill out a template sent in the back to you with like the name, the age, job experience, English level, and just a couple of questions
set up a direct hiring funnel that may even take like a week to get all the applicants
before I was just hiring and hoping they were good but you showed me like a real vetting process like the proper training SOPs Google forms
the VA we hired through the system she knew exactly what she was doing from since day one

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter hiring baseline (§8 outsourced). This is pure build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat team roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build these three filters into Palm's future chatter hiring SOP as Stage 1. The AP-score filter is onlinejobs.ph-specific and low-effort; include it as a default screen. Design the template with at least one open-ended question specifically to measure effort, not content.

new_findings.title: Price dynamically off purchase history

variants.claim: Setting price per fan from their purchase history, backing off after a no and pushing higher for proven whales, beats one fixed price for all.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard uses dynamic pricing read from each subscriber's unlock history via tools like Infloww: if a fan stalled at $65 he offers 17/27/47/59 next time, but if a fan will pay $150-$200 for what others pay $20 for he sells at that willingness, and he keeps explicit content premium-priced so perceived value stays high.

variants.claim: Operators charge different prices for identical content based on the fan's traffic source and proven spend, deliberately under-pricing lower-quality traffic.
variants.what_they_do: X-sourced subs (lower quality, free subs) tagged and sold PPVs at roughly half the price of IG subs; proven spenders and whales quoted higher anchors; price never discussed openly, instead gauged from chat, fan notes and tip-baiting ('show me what you think I'm worth').

sources.quote: you pick a different selling strategy for every single subscriber
the low price might make him not want to buy instead of buy... keep the content premium priced
you can target them with lower price content to increase your spend per sub
we use differential pricing to target the X subs at lower lower prices

palm_comparison.vs_us: No per-fan price discipline or perceived-value rule at Palm (§7 THIN). Direct gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a dynamic per-fan pricing rule keyed to unlock history, plus a floor that keeps explicit content premium-priced to protect perceived value.

new_findings.topic: Urgency and exclusivity framing for PPV
new_findings.title: Apply time-sensitive + VIP-exclusivity framing to every PPV offer

variants.claim: Framing PPV offers as time-limited and personally exclusive ('only for you') reliably increases conversion rates.
variants.what_they_do: Two distinct psychological levers are scripted: (1) urgency — 'only available for a few hours'; (2) exclusivity — 'I don't usually send this to just anyone but you're special.' Both are delivered conversationally so they feel organic, not like a sales blast.

variants.claim: Long, vivid PPV descriptions that pass a 'can you picture it with your eyes closed' imagination test and a 'does it arouse' boner test dramatically increase unlock rates because the sub is buying stimulation from the description itself, not the preview.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are required to write out full descriptions for every PPV — not 'here's a pic of my boobs babe' but a multi-sentence, immersive description of camera angle, positioning, what the model is doing, and what the sub should imagine. Two quality tests: (1) Imagination test — can someone visualize exactly what is in the content just from the words, with no preview? (2) Boner test — does the description itself arouse? If a male chatter reads it and finds it hot, it will likely work. Pre-writing descriptions for an entire set before the selling session begins is standard practice. This is treated as a core skill, not an afterthought.

sources.quote: make the offer feel exclusive and time sensitive example I have something special for you but it's only available for a few hours
I don't usually send this to just anyone but you're special I love talking to you
they are not vivid they do not stimulate the subscriber's imagination
does the description vividly describe what is in the content… will the description give the sub a boner when they read it

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal PPV pricing or sales-framing baseline exists at Palm (§7 GAP). The external chat team may or may not use these frames; Palm has no oversight of that language.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Include both urgency and exclusivity language templates in the PPV script library Palm will build for its in-house chat team. Also share these frames with the current outsourced team as a performance-improvement brief. Track conversion rate before/after to validate.

new_findings.topic: Fan segmentation — low vs high spenders
new_findings.title: Segment fans by max spend ($50 vs $120+) and use different opening tactics per tier

variants.claim: Low/non-spenders need fast, transactional, no-brainer openings, while high spenders need slower, personalized, emotionally invested approaches that reflect the higher ROI of putting time into them.
variants.what_they_do: Two distinct opening playbooks: (1) Low/non-spenders (<$50 max purchase): 'Two choices' illusion (free thing + heavily discounted PPV framed as a favor); 'Question game' (ask 2–3 preference questions about color/toy/angle, disappear briefly, return with a PPV 'chosen based on their answers'). Goal is just to get the first purchase and enter the loop. (2) High spenders (>$120 max purchase): 'Opinion opening' (frame a PPV as something the creator is nervous/unsure about, ask for their trusted opinion — if they object to price, say you're 'impartial' and it makes them more curious); 'Personalized PPV' for lapsed high spenders (manually send a high-quality video with a caption referencing their past purchases, their interests from fan notes, priced $150–200, with fan's name in the first 5 words of the last message sent).

variants.claim: Organizing the subscriber inbox into five explicit priority tiers ensures chatters always work highest-revenue conversations first and never waste time on non-spenders while whales wait.
variants.what_they_do: Priority order: (1) fans currently in a sext/purchase session, (2) fans being transitioned into a session (teasing phase), (3) new subscribers (unknown spend potential — test them), (4) known spenders/whales in relationship-building, (5) time-wasters — only addressed when inbox is empty. Agency adjusts exact ordering based on where their revenue concentrates.

sources.quote: if we were talking to, say, a low or non-spender, we want to be able to transition in a way that is fast and efficient and make that first pay-per-view seem like a no-brainer
it's pretty much exclusively for non-active high spenders. So people that used to spend a lot of money, but don't really talk anymore
number one in my opinion for everybody should be the ones that are currently in a sexting session
The last ones should be time wasters because they're just wasting our time

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting module (§9) that tracks spend data, but no documented segmented selling playbook or per-tier opener scripts. The data infrastructure exists; the chatter execution layer does not.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Map these two tiers onto Palm's existing Fan Tracker/whale-hunting data. For the in-house chat team, write two distinct opener script banks keyed to max-spend threshold. The 'question game' opener is particularly low-friction and could be A/B tested quickly once chatting is brought in-house. The lapsed-whale personalized PPV ($150–200, notes-referenced caption) is a high-ROI re-engagement tactic worth standardizing.

new_findings.title: Classify every creator A/B/C and allocate resources accordingly — cut C creators

variants.claim: Most agencies carry too many low-quality ('C') creators out of fear of losing gross revenue, which silently caps growth by stealing attention from better creators.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan classifies creators into three tiers: A = high upside, consistent content, coachable, scalable brand; B = potential, needs structure, worth developing; C = inconsistent, ignores feedback, creates chaos, low upside, drains team. A creators get premium attention and resources. B creators get active development. C creators get fixed, downgraded, or removed. The diagnostic question is: 'Knowing what I know now, would I sign this creator again today?' If no, there must be a reason she is still consuming company resources. He notes a 30k/month agency with 2 great creators beats a 100k/month agency with 14 nightmares.

variants.claim: Low-tier models produce low-tier results regardless of agency skill, so raising the minimum quality bar of creators signed is a prerequisite to scaling past $50K/month.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis explicitly rejects creators with low work ethic regardless of other factors. He qualifies inbound leads using story-driven case-study content (e.g. 'how we took a creator from 0 to 40K') that pre-filters serious creators, rather than vanity result screenshots. The content itself does the qualification work before any call.

sources.quote: the creator audit is where you classify your roster into A, B, and C
knowing what I know now, would I sign this creator again today? That is the last question. That's very important.
most agencies carry way too many Creators, and they keep them out of the fear of losing their revenue number
if you keep acquiring low tier models, you're going to get low tier results because you got low tier work ethic and it's not going to scale

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a `Creators` table with status tracking and onboarding workflow (§6), but no documented creator-tier classification, no systematic review cadence, and no written policy on when to downgrade or drop a creator. This is a GAP (§9 retention, §7 pricing/deal structure). Palm's creator acquisition is currently reactive/Josh-DM-led (§6) with no quality filter documented.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Implement a quarterly creator audit in the admin portal: list all active creators, assign A/B/C rating based on gross profit, content compliance rate, team hours consumed, and a 'would sign again?' flag. Set explicit policies: A creators get priority chat team allocation and content pipeline slots; B creators get a 60-day improvement plan; C creators get a downgrade notice or off-boarding. Pair with the profitability scorecard (finding above) to make ratings data-driven rather than subjective.

new_findings.title: Every reel needs hook, retention, reward; 2-3/day across content pillars

variants.claim: Reels are the primary growth lever and each is engineered with a 3-second hook, mid retention and a payoff, produced at 2-3/day split across a few fixed content pillars.
variants.what_they_do: Treats reels as the top of the IG pecking order (reels > posts > stories > funnel); structures each as hook (first 3s) / retention / reward (e.g. an ugly-to-hot transformation); defines ~3 niche content pillars and split-tests them at 2-3 reels/day, doubling down on winners and cutting losers; moved from static text-on-image reels to 'clean motion' video.

variants.claim: Brand equity decays with every silent day, so consistent daily multi-post output across platforms is required maintenance, not optional.
variants.what_they_do: Posts 6x/day across IG and TikTok personally; frames missed days as compound interest in reverse where creators, algorithms, and the market all forget; only tracks posting consistency (plus inbounds, repeat viewers, proof) as a real KPI, ignoring vanity metrics.

sources.quote: Every reel should have a hook, a retention and a reward
a minimum of two quality reels per day
I post six times a day
every time that you stop posting for a week, your brand equity decays

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's inspo pipeline scores viral reels and decides what to film (§1 MATURE) but does not codify per-reel hook/retention/reward structure or a pillar split-test cadence; real-creator cadence is a gap (§1).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a reel-structure standard (hook/retention/reward) and a pillar-based split-test posting cadence to the inspo pipeline output for real creators; complements Palm's existing scoring engine.

new_findings.title: Let AI sort and convert new subs; route spenders to human chatters

variants.claim: The emerging chatting meta is a hybrid where AI handles new-subscriber volume and identifies real spenders, then hands those fans to human chatters for long-term girlfriend-experience retention.
variants.what_they_do: Has AI run all new subscribers and complete the first script to separate spenders from time-wasters, then moves real spenders to a human chatter who gives a daily girlfriend experience to a small client list (~20 fans each); reports ~70% unlock rate and a single whale spending $50k in 4 months.

variants.claim: Cloning a real signed creator into an AI model and mixing AI with real content lets an agency keep OnlyFans conversion while multiplying volume.
variants.what_they_do: Take 20-30 photos of a real signed creator, train an AI model on her face/body, generate motion-control videos, then post a real+AI mix that all funnels to her real OnlyFans (no AI sold past the wall). One model films 35-40 videos/week; AI fills the other 40-45, enabling 4+ accounts.

variants.claim: Full-AI creators are forced onto less-trusted third-party platforms where conversion drops 30-50% versus OnlyFans, making hybrid (real OF) structurally more profitable.
variants.what_they_do: Argues full AI cannot use OnlyFans (ID verification, no AI content allowed) so they go to Fansly/Fanvue/Fanfix where saved-credit-card trust is absent and conversion tanks; hybrid keeps everything on OnlyFans.

sources.quote: I will let the AI deal with all the new subscribers... so that it can differentiate between the actual spenders and the time wasters
we want the chatter to specifically just focus on a specific number amount of clients
you take a real creator and you generate your own AI profile of that creator
Your conversion rates will literally drop 30 to 50% just by doing that platform rather than Only Fans

palm_comparison.vs_us: Defines the target architecture for Palm's AI-chatting project and future in-house team (§8 build-toward): AI for volume/sorting, humans for whale retention.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design Palm's chat roadmap as a hybrid: AI for new-sub volume and spender-sorting, human chatters owning small VIP client lists for retention, with unlock-rate as a tracked KPI.

new_findings.title: Set hard minimum PPV prices: $19.99 images, $39.99 videos, $300 customs

variants.claim: Selling PPV content below ~$20 for photos and ~$40 for videos trains fans to see the creator as cheap and destroys long-term monetization.
variants.what_they_do: This agency enforces hard price floors: no image PPV under $19.99, no video PPV under $39.99, no custom under $300. Low pricing is treated as a disqualifier, not a conversion tool. The logic is anchoring — cheap prices signal low value and attract fans who won't spend more, wasting chatter time for minimal return (framed as bad 'cash-for-effort' ratio).

variants.claim: Pricing PPV content by video length with a hard floor of 1 minute and a practical ceiling of ~$20 maximizes purchase rate across different fan spending levels.
variants.what_they_do: Short or clothed videos: under $8. Longer or more explicit videos: $8–$20. Hard rule: never charge for content under 1 minute — that goes free. Photos posted free to subscribers (subscription covers them); photos only charged if custom. Sends a mix of price points so lower-budget fans still convert on cheaper items while high-spenders can hit the $20–$25 ceiling.

sources.quote: you're now going to be selling pictures for no less than $19.99 each
Customs I'm not joking no less than $300

if I send out a shorter video or like a twerking video or I'm clothed then it's somewhere under like eight dollars and if it's a little bit more than that then it's somewhere over eight dollars always under I would say twenty dollars

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model or price floors (§7, explicit GAP). No internal baseline exists for what creators should charge for images, videos, or customs.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Establish Palm's standard PPV price floor as agency policy: ~$20 minimum for images, ~$40 minimum for videos, ~$300 for customs. Document this in the creator onboarding materials and share with the external chat team as a standing instruction. This is a zero-cost, immediate revenue lever.

new_findings.title: Five-tier chat priority queue: active sext session first, time-wasters last

variants.claim: Organizing the subscriber inbox into five explicit priority tiers ensures chatters always work highest-revenue conversations first and never waste time on non-spenders while whales wait.
variants.what_they_do: Priority order: (1) fans currently in a sext/purchase session, (2) fans being transitioned into a session (teasing phase), (3) new subscribers (unknown spend potential — test them), (4) known spenders/whales in relationship-building, (5) time-wasters — only addressed when inbox is empty. Agency adjusts exact ordering based on where their revenue concentrates.

variants.claim: When managing multiple accounts simultaneously, chatters should prioritize responses to high-spending or long-tenure subscribers over the general queue.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters explicitly rank their open conversations: whales and loyal long-term members get first responses. Low-spend or new subscribers are handled after, often with templated messages to maintain efficiency without abandoning them.

sources.quote: number one in my opinion for everybody should be the ones that are currently in a sexting session
The last ones should be time wasters because they're just wasting our time
prioritize high value chats focus on subscribers who frequently spend money or longtime members

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a whale-hunting module (§9) and Fan Tracker CRM but no documented real-time chat prioritization queue. The outsourced chat team may have their own system, but Palm has no visibility into it.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team, implement this queue as a visible status layer in the Fan Tracker CRM. Consider surfacing active-session and new-subscriber flags in the whale-hunting dashboard (§9) so the chat manager can monitor live priority distribution across creators.

new_findings.topic: Commonality building as the core sales engine
new_findings.title: Three-tier commonality system to manufacture emotional bond

variants.claim: Building plausible commonalities with subscribers — using a tiered system from direct 'me too' to loose tangential connections — is the single most important driver of repeat purchasing.
variants.what_they_do: After qualification, chatters build commonalities using three levels: Level 1 ('me too') — chatter claims to share the same trait directly (e.g., 'I also study psychology'). Used sparingly because it's hard to track across many subs. Level 2 ('I know someone who') — chatter links via a third party ('My little brother studies psychology'). Common but overuse feels weird. Level 3 (tangential connection) — chatter links the sub's trait to something loosely related ('I read a Harvard Business Review article about students who study under 2 hours a night…'). This is the core technique — it's harder to fake, opens new conversational threads, and doesn't require the chatter to claim personal overlap. The goal is to give a 'plausible reason' for emotional connection so that future sales requests don't feel transactional.

variants.claim: The girlfriend experience (parasocial connection, scripted live-feeling sells, no PPV spam) drives the highest and most durable chatting revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Optimizes the OF wall to feel like 'Snapchatting,' uses 7-10 stage scripts with verification images, never initiates sexual content (lets the fan initiate), handles broke fans with ego-protecting lines, and forbids PPV spamming across the whole team.

sources.quote: building commonalities this is going to be the most important section in this presentation
oh really that's interesting I was reading an article in the Harvard Business review and it said that students who study under 2 hours a night do 50% worse
the GF experience, the only thing that I try to run in OFM

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward. Notably, the 'plausible reason for validation' logic is essentially applied sales psychology that Palm could encode as a training principle, not just a chatting script.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Make Level 3 commonality building the centrepiece of Palm's in-house chatter training. Create a worked-example library organized by profession/hobby (e.g., 'if sub says he's a nurse, here are three Level 3 connections'). Pair this with the qualification CRM data so chatters can prep connections before re-engaging a returning sub.

new_findings.topic: Reddit content strategy vs. Instagram content strategy
new_findings.title: Use high-volume 7/10 static images on Reddit — never repurpose IG hook-driven reels

variants.claim: Reddit rewards posting volume of decent static/short-clip content across many subreddits, not the single viral hook-driven reel optimised for Instagram; ten 7/10 images outperform two 10/10 videos on Reddit.
variants.what_they_do: Agency repurposes a bank of ~100 photos per creator to sustain a full year of Reddit posting without new shoots. Videos used on Reddit are simple (10-15 sec, body-focused, no hook/narrative) and the same clip is reposted across 100 subreddits simultaneously. No time-of-day optimisation — just maximum post frequency spread across subreddits. Goal is aggregate impressions across subreddits, not a single viral post.

variants.claim: Content quality is the single most important Reddit variable — good content can overcome mediocre subreddits, but bad content fails even in perfect subreddits.
variants.what_they_do: Start with a niche (e.g. feet), find subreddits by searching and crawling competitor model pages. Post in each; if a subreddit doesn't reach ~20+ upvotes, swap it for a new one. Simultaneously test content by finding top-performing posts within target subreddits and asking the creator to recreate them with a personal twist. Scale to 20 subreddits/account then plateau for a month before expanding further.

sources.quote: give me a hundred pictures like like have a creator give me 100 pictures and I can promote her for the rest of the year with those 100 pictures
I would much rather have 10 pieces of content that are like seven out of 10, then I would have three pieces of content, two or three pieces of content that are 10 out of 10
we basically test the subreddit… if it doesn't get like 20 plus uploads a day then we just change them out for new ones

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content system (§1) is engineered entirely around IG hook-driven reels — viral scoring, transcript analysis, inspo-driven filming. That pipeline produces the wrong content type for Reddit. Palm also has thin OF vault content (§2); Reddit uses a middle-ground asset type (SFW-ish or soft NSFW stills/clips) that neither pipeline currently covers.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a third content tier for Reddit: simple, high-quality still photos and 10-15 sec body-focused clips. These can be captured as a small add-on during existing shoots. Store in Airtable as a separate asset type ('Reddit Assets'). Do not route IG reels to Reddit — they are wrong-format and waste the hook work.

new_findings.title: Convert founder gut-reactions into SOPs so the team executes your standards without you

variants.claim: Every time the founder instinctively knows how they would handle a situation, that instinct should be written into an SOP so the team follows the same standard without requiring the founder's involvement.
variants.what_they_do: TDM's framework: 'your intuition, the way you feel, should become the rules of the business.' If the founder would handle a creator issue a certain way, that becomes a documented framework the team is trained on. The goal is to remove the founder from execution while preserving their operating philosophy.

variants.claim: Learn each part of the business personally and turn it into an SOP before delegating, so hires run a documented process.
variants.what_they_do: Owner did outreach/closing/onboarding/marketing/chatting himself first, then wrote an SOP doc + Loom video for each task so new hires self-train from the document instead of hours of live training. Hires for hunger/reliability/coachability over experience (experienced people resist the system). Tracks KPIs on every function (DMs sent, replies, calls booked, clients closed, revenue per chatter).

sources.quote: if you feel that, oh, I would deal with a creator's issue like this, then that needs to become an SOP
you funnel all of that traffic over to Only Fans. And then the second thing is the chatting.
One of the first things that you need to do is connect their Only Fans account to your Inflow.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's systems are strong on the content pipeline (§1) but thin on creator-facing ops, chatting protocols, and acquisition handling (§6, §8). The SOP layer for creator management and issue resolution is not documented internally.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Run a structured 'intuition capture' session: Josh walks through the 10 most common creator/fan/operational situations he handles and dictates how he'd resolve each. Evan documents these as v1 SOPs in Airtable or Notion. Prioritize creator onboarding issues, escalation handling, and any workflow currently bottlenecked on Josh.

new_findings.title: 90-min Loom training video with embedded accountability checkpoints to filter non-completers

variants.claim: Embedding hidden checkpoints (emoji signals, mid-video questions) inside a mandatory 90-minute onboarding video filters out candidates who won't follow through — before they ever touch a live fan account.
variants.what_they_do: After passing all screening stages, selected candidates receive a 90+ minute video covering: how OnlyFans works, how the CRM (Creator Hero) works, scripts, hotkeys, and job structure. The video includes 2–3 embedded accountability checks — e.g., 'send me a cherry emoji if you watched to here' or answer specific questions after viewing. Anyone who fails to complete or respond is cut. Rationale: if a chatter won't watch a Loom on Day 1, they won't perform reliably in month 2.

variants.claim: Recording yourself doing each task as a Loom video (rather than written docs) is the most reliable way to train hires and remove misinterpretation.
variants.what_they_do: Films a Loom once per task and reuses it forever; pairs Loom with backup documents for objections; uses live chatter monitoring on first 1-2 shifts plus 'trust but verify' checks for the first 1-2 weeks.

sources.quote: send them like a 90-minute plus video explaining the entire job... include a couple of accountability checks
if someone isn't even watching a Loom video you sent them at their first day on the job, you can imagine how he's going to work in a month from now
Loom recordings

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no in-house chatter onboarding (§8). This tactic is directly relevant to the AI chatting roadmap project and in-house chat team buildout. Palm already uses Loom-style documentation in other workflows — this pattern is familiar.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design Palm's future chatter onboarding as a video-first, checkpoint-gated flow. Use a single 60–90 min Loom (or similar) covering OF mechanics, Palm's CRM/tools, scripts, and KPIs, with 2–3 embedded compliance signals. Treat failure to complete as an automatic disqualification.

new_findings.title: IG mother/slave split: main account accumulates reach; disposable slave holds the OF link

variants.claim: Because AI model accounts are vulnerable to Instagram takedowns, you protect compound growth by keeping the main posting account clean and pushing the monetization link through a separate 'slave' account tagged in the bio.
variants.what_they_do: Main (mother) account posts content normally, builds followers and watch time, and tags a second 'slave' account in bio. The slave account holds the link-in-bio to Fanvue/OF. When Instagram removes the slave account (expected), the mother account survives and keeps its audience; a new slave is swapped in. Real human creators avoid this because they're ID-verified and harder to ban.

variants.claim: Placing the OF link only on the main creator page (not on satellite/funnel accounts) reduces satellite account suspension rate to approximately 1-in-20.
variants.what_they_do: Satellite accounts never carry any OF or external link in their bios. Their sole job is to drive IG profile visits to the creator's main account (or a set of 5 intermediate accounts). The OF link lives exclusively at the final destination page. This separation keeps satellite accounts off Meta's adult-content radar and dramatically lowers ban rates.

sources.quote: we're going to take our models on a second account and then put that in bio
This is the mother account, main account, and then the slave account is where we take it at
in none of these accounts' bios, we're putting that link... we're just funneling to other Instagram pages, so our accounts can stay way safer

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI persona warmup system (§3/§4) uses hardware-isolated GrapheneOS devices and aged accounts but does not document a mother/slave split specifically to protect link-in-bio from takedowns. Palm's real-creator accounts would not need this (ID-verified), but AI personas would.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the AI persona line (Amelia, Katie Rosie, Brielle — §3), implement the mother/slave IG architecture: main persona account never holds the OF/Beacons link directly; a linked secondary account absorbs takedown risk. Build a fast slave-account replacement SOP into the warmup playbook (§4).

new_findings.topic: Tiered commission escalation to reduce signing friction
new_findings.title: Start at 20-30% commission and escalate by milestone to earn the full split

variants.claim: New agencies without proven results should offer a tiered commission structure — low percentage at signing, escalating to 50% only after hitting defined revenue milestones — to reduce creator risk and incentivize agency performance.
variants.what_they_do: One speaker describes his first deal: 30% upfront, moving to 40% at $15k/month, then 50/50 above $30k. The logic is explicit: 'I didn't know anything about this game' and going in at 50% without proof is unjustifiable for the creator. The escalator also creates aligned incentives — the agency is motivated to grow the account because their own take increases. A complementary tactic: for established creators already making (e.g.) $5k/month, guarantee that floor no matter what the agency does in month one, demonstrating good faith.

variants.claim: Stating '50% revenue split' is not a real offer — agencies must articulate specific outcomes (more time, more money, viral growth) to differentiate, because every competitor leads with the same split.
variants.what_they_do: Agency frames its pitch around two core creator pain points: (1) inability to grow new fans and (2) lack of time to manage everything. The offer is sold as 'save your time, make more money' with concrete evidence (screenshots of revenue growth from existing clients). The 50% figure is justified by showing the creator went from $8K to $108K/month — the split is irrelevant when absolute earnings quadruple.

sources.quote: my first Creator I went in at 30% and I said at 30% I will start if we get past 15K it'll move to 40 and if I can get you past 30k we'll go 50/50 from there moving forward
what not to do is... my agency takes 50% blah blah. This is not a real offer. It is instead a revenue split. There is no value to that

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % at onboarding as a fixed field (§7), but there is no documented tiered/milestone-based commission model or floor-guarantee tactic for established creators. No internal baseline exists for how Palm's splits are structured or justified. This is a §7 gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Develop a documented commission framework with two tracks: (1) New creator / unproven account — start at 30-35%, escalate to 50% upon hitting defined monthly revenue threshold (suggest $10k/month, consistent with the 3-month target the speakers reference). (2) Established creator already earning — offer a monthly floor guarantee equal to their current earnings; charge full split only on revenue above that floor in month one. Codify both in the onboarding contract wizard.

new_findings.title: Start with exactly 2 clients — not 1, not 3+

variants.claim: New agencies should aim to sign two clients simultaneously: one as a hedge if the other underperforms, but no more than two to avoid operational overwhelm before systems are proven.
variants.what_they_do: Prescribes a deliberate two-client start: if one relationship fails the other provides continuity, while capping at two prevents the operator from over-extending before they have SOPs, chatters, or reliable traffic in place.

variants.claim: Beginners who try to sign many clients simultaneously disappoint everyone and burn out; mastering operations on one client first produces better outcomes.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan explicitly advises new agency operators to sign a single creator, test all marketing and chatting strategies on that one account, and only expand once they know what works. He argues that without in-house chatters (which beginners can't afford) and without proven marketing, having five-plus clients is unmanageable and leads to poor results across the board.

sources.quote: you should ideally aim to sign two clients from the get-go. If you just have one, you can stay with one, of course, but in most cases, one of them might not work out
focus on one client to begin with because a lot of beginners will try and just like scatter gun and try and get as many clients as they can thinking that that is going to solve their problems

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented guidance on optimal portfolio size at different stages of agency maturity (§6 GAP). No scaling model or client-count-to-capacity ratio is written down.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define explicit capacity tiers in Palm's onboarding/ops documentation: e.g., Tier 0 (founding) = 1-2 creators, Tier 1 = up to 5 once chatting is delegated, Tier 2 = 10+ once a full team is in place. Use this to gate new creator signings against current team bandwidth.

new_findings.title: Build a Telegram-sourced S4S network before signing creators to pre-load launch traffic

variants.claim: Developing a network of same-size trans creators and agencies for mutual shoutouts on OF, IG, Telegram, TikTok, and X before launch allows an agency to spike a new creator to tens of thousands of fans on day one.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner joins as many OFM Telegram groups as possible, posts availability for S4S and GG collabs, and records all contacts in a spreadsheet. Matching logic: pages of roughly equal fan count do S4S (mutual shoutout); significantly larger pages are approached for paid GGs instead. Pre-launch, the agency pre-schedules promos from the entire network so that on launch day the new creator immediately receives a surge of fans. Once internal pages grow, new creator launches are boosted by the agency's own portfolio of existing creators ('internal boosting'), eliminating the need to pay for early GGs.

variants.claim: An agency entering a new niche should develop a promotional network of at least 10–20 creators and agencies before signing any creator, so that traffic infrastructure is ready on day one.
variants.what_they_do: Before signing a single trans creator, the agency owner spends time in Telegram groups building relationships with 10–20 trans creators and several agencies that manage trans creators. Pre-launch, the agency pre-schedules GGs and S4S deals with this network so that as soon as a creator is onboarded, a surge of guaranteed fans (e.g. 10,000 on day one via $5K in GGs) can be deployed immediately. This inverts the usual sequence of 'sign creator, then figure out traffic.'

sources.quote: you're going to gain most of your network through Telegram groups
when you onboard new creators, you pre-schedule promos from your entire network and all of your internal pages as well, boosting new pages to $100,000 in the first month
before you sign any trans girls, I'd say start developing your network — get at least like 10–20 trans creators that you're in contact with
as soon as you launch, have all of them pre-schedule everything so you have like $5,000 in guaranteed gains coming in

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented S4S or network-building playbook (§5 GAP). The system is entirely absent from Palm's current ops baseline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Assign a part-time 'network getter' task (could be Josh or a VA) to join 5–10 OFM Telegram groups and begin logging trans-creator and agency contacts into an Airtable table (Name, Platform, Fan Count, Contact Method, Last Outreach, Deal Type). Even if Palm doesn't operate trans creators today, the mechanics of S4S and launch-day pre-scheduling are transferable to any niche and should be piloted on the next real-creator launch.

new_findings.title: Discord live call with screen-share: WPM test + English test + physical setup camera reveal

variants.claim: A live Discord call with screen-sharing — covering typing speed, English proficiency, and a live camera reveal of the candidate's physical workspace — surfaces reliability signals that asynchronous tests cannot.
variants.what_they_do: Stage 3 live assessment via Discord with screen-share: (1) words-per-minute typing test (candidates will cheat; if you can't catch it, they earned it); (2) live English proficiency test using any free online tool; (3) ask the candidate to turn their camera and film their desk/setup in real time. The desk reveal is flagged as unique and highly valuable — an organized vs. disorganized desk is treated as a proxy for how they'll manage their work. Philosophy: 'how you do one thing is how you do everything.'

variants.claim: A 10-question situational/emotional intelligence test sent to all shortlisted applicants in a single group chat efficiently identifies candidates with natural sales instinct without requiring individual calls.
variants.what_they_do: Create a group chat (WhatsApp or Telegram) with all applicants who passed Stage 1. Send a Google Docs with 10 situational questions designed to reveal emotional intelligence and objection-handling instinct — not chatting skill. Example: 'You own a vegan restaurant and someone asks for steak — what do you say?' The 'right' answer reframes the objection and redirects to an alternative rather than refusing. This filters out poor communicators before investing time in live interviews.

sources.quote: let them hop on a Discord live call with like a shared screen... words per minute test, how fast they can type
ask them, 'Hey, quickly turn your camera around and show me your setup.' Because it shows you so much about what type of person they are.
create a group chat with all of the applicants and you're going to give them their first test... a situational awareness test
you are the owner of a vegan restaurant and someone comes in asking for real meat, for a steak. What would you tell that person?

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal live vetting process (§8 outsourced). The desk/setup reveal is a novel, zero-cost signal not commonly used. Build-toward for in-house chat hiring.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Include the three-part live Discord assessment as Stage 3 in Palm's future chatter hiring SOP. The setup camera reveal is especially low-effort to implement and provides a soft organizational signal. Document acceptable vs. disqualifying desk conditions loosely to avoid over-filtering.

new_findings.topic: AI reel production — full synthetic content workflow
new_findings.title: Screenshot → GPT prompt → image gen → animation → CapCut caption: full AI reel pipeline

variants.claim: A fully AI-generated reel (no face swap, no real footage) avoids copyright and brand risk while still achieving viral reach when built from reverse-engineered prompts of high-performing inspo reels.
variants.what_they_do: Operator (1) identifies high-performing AI content on IG (targeting 100k–1M view reels, motion reels preferred), (2) screenshots the best frame, (3) feeds screenshot to Grok or ChatGPT to generate a detailed image prompt, (4) pastes prompt into Kling/MidJourney/CR to generate the image, (5) animates it in Kling, (6) adds captions in CapCut, (7) posts one reel/day for 10 days. At 10 posts they analyze which performed best and scale that template with a 'trial reel strategy' (20 reels/day).

variants.claim: The key to making an AI-animated reel go viral is identifying the specific physical action in the original viral reel and using that exact action as the animation prompt, rather than a generic description.
variants.what_they_do: When a viral reel is identified (e.g., 1M views), the operator distills the core action ('girl is doing a backflip') as the animation prompt fed into the AI tool (Kling). They then ask the AI tool to refine and make the prompt 'as realistic as possible.' The first frame of the recreated image is animated with this action prompt.

sources.quote: screenshot and ask Grok or ChatGPT to make a detailed prompt of this image and with the prompt you're going to paste it on Kling, MidJourney, or CR
post one reel a day for 10 days… you're going to analyze the data for 10 days… see which one performs the best
the secret prompt is what the original reel is doing to make it viral… if the girl is doing a backflip the secret prompt is going to be the girl is doing a backflip
ask the deep fake to refine the prompt and make it as much realistic as possible

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's §1 pipeline is strong on inspo-driven ideation for real creators but does not include a fully synthetic reel production track. Palm does make AI social content for some real creators (§0 note) and has AI persona infrastructure (§3), but a codified screenshot→GPT-prompt→image-gen→animate→post workflow is not documented. This is AI-persona-relevant but the prompt-reverse-engineering technique is transferable to real-creator content ideation too.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the AI persona line (§3, build-toward): document this 6-step synthetic reel workflow as a standing SOP. For real creators: evaluate whether the 'screenshot inspo frame → GPT detailed prompt → recreate visually' technique can improve brief quality in step 5 (Promote as filming idea) of the existing §1 pipeline. The 10-day test-then-scale cadence (post 10, pick winner, scale) is a low-cost experiment framework worth formalizing.

new_findings.topic: Creator brand identity vs. volume posting
new_findings.title: Stop volume-posting; define creator persona/brand before scaling content

variants.claim: Posting high volume of content (10 TikToks/day, 10 IG Reels/day) plateaus revenue at ~$10K/mo; defining who the creator actually is — her vibe, niche, audience, and what makes her different — is what breaks past that ceiling.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin forced Barry to stop posting entirely and answer 'who is this model?' before resuming. They built a persona/brand sheet for each creator covering identity, vibe, niche, and differentiator. Content strategy only resumes once that identity is locked. They report LTV increases of 20–30% and improved CVR after implementing this.

variants.claim: Learning to identify the precise moment in a video responsible for views or comments — not just copying formats — is the analytical skill that scales organic revenue past $10K/mo.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin reviews videos with operators on calls and annotates specific timestamps: 'this point in the video is why it got views; this point is why it got comments.' Over 4–6 months of repeated exposure, operators internalize the pattern and can evaluate new content without needing external input. It is described as time + experience compressed through deliberate coaching.

sources.quote: Stop posting for a second and tell me who the model actually is
figuring out her identity such as her vibe her audience her niche and then yeah just what makes her different
every single one of our calls I would try and underline exactly what made a video go viral like why some videos worked why some didn't
you can look at a video and be like this point in the video is why I got so many views or this point in the video is why I got so many comments

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) is data-driven and inspo-scraped — strong on WHAT to film virally, but the baseline doc flags no formalized creator persona/brand identity step before content ideation. We may be optimizing delivery of content without first defining who the creator is to her fans, which is the same trap Barry fell into.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'Creator Identity' step zero to Palm's 8-step content pipeline: before any inspo scraping or filming brief is issued for a new creator, complete a persona sheet (niche, vibe, audience archetype, key differentiators, tone of voice). Gate content production on this being signed off. This is especially relevant for real-creator onboarding (§6) where it can be captured at the form wizard stage.

new_findings.title: Request a specific content mix upfront: sexting sets, PPV bundles, mass-message clips, and verification posts

variants.claim: Agencies must tell models exactly what content types they need at onboarding — not generically 'more content' — so all traffic and monetization strategies can be activated immediately.
variants.what_they_do: During the onboarding call they specify four distinct content buckets needed upfront: (1) sexting/script sets for DM selling, (2) PPV bundles and standalone videos for mass messages, (3) SFW promotional content for Reddit, Twitter, and Instagram, (4) verification posts for subreddits that require them. They also provide a weekly content schedule so the model knows what to film the following week.

variants.claim: Agencies should proactively coach creators on production quality during onboarding rather than waiting for bad content to arrive and damage relationships.
variants.what_they_do: They explicitly advise creators on: (1) lighting — minimum ring light or studio light for long-form video, softer/natural for sexting sets to avoid an overly-produced look; (2) audio — no background noise; (3) background — clean, uncluttered; (4) variety — multiple outfits, angles, lingerie styles, and outdoor locations. This is delivered as part of the onboarding process, not as reactive feedback.

sources.quote: scripts so like live content that you could sell via sexting sets in the DMS for of you also need to have ppv bundles and and videos that you can use to mass message out
providing some sort of content schedule to them on a weekly basis as to what you would likely need for that following week
lighting is probably the number one most important thing when it comes to taking high quality content make sure that you're using at least a ring light
make sure that the background of the videos and the pictures and everything doesn't have a bunch like a mess or Garbage

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) is inspo-driven and strong for social/SFW content. However §2 flags that OF vault/PPV content has no systematic production pipeline. There is no documented process for requesting specific content buckets at onboarding or providing weekly content briefs to creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a content-brief template to the onboarding call checklist: enumerate the four buckets (sexting sets, PPV bundles, SFW promo, verification posts) and send a weekly 'content request' message to each creator. This closes the §2 OF-vault gap and connects to Palm's existing social pipeline for the SFW bucket.

new_findings.topic: Cloud phone farm for IG account amplification
new_findings.title: Operate 200–300 cloud-based IG accounts to funnel traffic to creator main pages

variants.claim: Running hundreds of warmed-up Instagram accounts via cloud phones on automated schedules generates 50–200 additional subs per day per creator while insulating the main account from bans.
variants.what_they_do: Agency spins up 200–300 Instagram accounts on cloud-hosted real phones. Automations handle: feed scrolling, liking, Reel posting, phone-number verification — all to mimic organic human behavior and avoid suspensions. None of these satellite accounts carry the OF link in bio; they funnel only to the creator's main IG page (or to 5 intermediate accounts). The OF link lives only on the main page. Suspension rate drops to ~1-in-20 vs. higher rates when the OF link is in satellite bios. If main account is banned, warmed satellite accounts are handed to the creator to restart on.

sources.quote: we set up hundreds of Instagram accounts... you can run everything off the cloud
In none of these accounts' bios, we're putting that link... we're just funneling to other Instagram pages
if we have 200 phones for one creator, and we're literally pulling one subscriber per day from each of the phones, then that's 200 subs a day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature AI-account warmup system for AI personas (§3/§4) using GrapheneOS Pixels and Publer, but this is for synthetic creator accounts, not a satellite-funnel network for real creators. Palm has no documented equivalent for real-creator traffic amplification (§4 🔲 GAP — real-creator warmup/cadence not formalized; §5 🔲 GAP — traffic strategy not documented).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Evaluate adopting a scaled cloud-phone funnel for real creators. Palm's existing hardware-isolated warmup infrastructure (GrapheneOS, SIM inventory, Publer) gives a meaningful head start — the playbook could be adapted. Key decisions: (1) cloud phone provider selection, (2) automation tooling for feed warm-up, (3) funnel topology (satellite → main IG → OF). Start small (20–30 accounts for one creator) before scaling to 200+. Flag as high-priority given current IG instability signals.

new_findings.title: Every team member must own a single measurable number — vague roles produce vague performance

variants.claim: Any metric that no one on the team explicitly owns will default back to the founder, making him the bottleneck and preventing scale.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan assigns a specific owned number to every role: setter owns qualified booked calls; closer owns cash collected; chatter manager owns chat revenue and quality; account manager owns creator retention and content compliance; creative director owns post volume and content performance; ops manager owns scorecard completion and weekly execution rhythm. He uses a 5-level accountability ladder: (1) wait to be told, (2) ask what to do, (3) recommend and act, (4) act then inform, (5) full ownership / exceptions only. Chatter managers should be at level 3-4; ops/sales managers at level 5. The coaching method to move someone from level 2→3: 'Before you come to me with a problem, bring me the problem, three options, and your recommendation.'

sources.quote: Every person on your team should own a number because any number nobody owns defaults back to you
Level three, recommend act. These people, they come to you and say, here's the problem. Here's the options. Here's my recommendation. Do you agree?
the setter owns qualified calls, the closer owns cash collected, the chatter manager owns chat revenue and quality

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has defined Clerk roles (super_admin, admin, editor, ai_editor, chat_manager, creator) but these are access-control roles, not outcome-ownership definitions (§10 GAP). There are no documented KPIs, scorecards, or accountability levels per role. The chat team is outsourced (§8), so the chatter manager role isn't yet internal, but the account manager, editor, and Amin (manual posting) roles have no documented owned numbers.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For each internal Palm role, define one owned number and the accountability level it should operate at: editor → content posted on schedule + revision rate (level 3-4); Amin/posting contractor → on-time post compliance rate (level 3); future chat manager → PPV conversion rate + response time (level 4-5). Add these to a simple weekly scorecard reviewed by Josh/Evan. This is foundational infrastructure before Palm scales headcount further (§10 GAP).

new_findings.topic: Quality-first creator sourcing and red-flag filtering
new_findings.title: Identify shared traits of best creators and make acquisition deliberately pickier

variants.claim: Fulfillment problems start at acquisition — a creator who won't send content or holds unrealistic expectations was a selection failure, not a delivery failure.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan runs an acquisition audit asking: where do the best creators come from (outbound, inbound, referrals, IG, switching agencies, existing creator networks)? What traits do the best creators share (existing social proof, consistent posting history, strong personality, existing audience, responsiveness, coachability, good on camera)? Which red flags are being ignored at signing? He distinguishes acquisition as a quality game vs. volume game depending on deal structure: pure volume only makes sense if selling a low-ticket course ($497) at scale with upsells; for a full-service agency the quality game dominates. He argues most agencies sign out of desperation to grow the inflow number and then complain about fulfillment 30 days later.

sources.quote: most agencies are nowhere near picky enough. They're so desperate to sign creators that they just ignore any of the red flags and then complain about fulfillment 30 days later
fulfillment starts at acquisition. You cannot outdeliver a bad creator selection forever
acquisition is a quality game or a volume game depending on the business model, depending on the deal structure

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is currently Josh-DM-led with no documented qualification criteria, scoring rubric, or red-flag checklist (§6 GAP). The onboarding form wizard captures name, email, commission %, state, contract — but nothing about creator quality signals. This is one of Palm's biggest identified gaps (§6).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a creator qualification scorecard used before any call is booked: existing monthly OF revenue, social following size and growth rate, content posting consistency (check IG grid), responsiveness in initial DMs, niche fit, willingness to follow direction (ask directly), and whether they've had a previous agency (and why they left). Score each prospect. Set a minimum threshold to proceed to a close call. Add a 'red flags observed' field to the `Creators` Airtable record. This directly addresses §6 GAP and will reduce C-creator drag identified in the retention finding above.

new_findings.topic: Chatting ratio KPI and chatter hire threshold
new_findings.title: Target 1:10 sub-to-chat revenue ratio; hire chatters only after reaching $2–5K/month agency revenue

variants.claim: A 1:10 ratio of subscription revenue to DM/tip revenue is the core chatting KPI, and chatters should not be hired until the agency earns $2–5K/month to ensure they're affordable.
variants.what_they_do: Chatting ratio defined as: for every $1 in subscription revenue, target $10 in DM+tip revenue. Benchmarks: 1:6 = decent, 1:8 = good, 1:10 = great, 1:15 = excellent. Hire VA chatters at $3–$5/hour + commission once agency hits $2–5K/month. Manage chatters via Discord. Welcome message framework: thank sub, ask their name, build rapport → send teasers → sell PPVs → upsell. Chatting is treated as a strategy, not ad-hoc.

variants.claim: Chatting performance should be measured as a ratio of message/tip revenue to subscription revenue, with elite teams targeting roughly 1:9 to 1:10.
variants.what_they_do: Calculates (messages + tips) / subscriptions; reports turning a ~1:1 ratio creator into 1:10+, and uses the ratio gap on creator calls to show lost revenue (e.g. a 1:3 vs 1:9 difference of thousands/month).

sources.quote: for every $1 you have in the chats, you want to have $10 in the or in the subs. You want to have $10 in the chats. So we stick to 1 to 10
